turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
1. Standardize categories: For the "Age," "Work Experience," and "Education Level" columns, make sure all responses are in the same format. For example, change "18-24" to "18-24 years."

2. Correct inconsistencies in text responses: For the "Country," "Industry," and "City" columns, standardize the responses. For example, change "USA" to "United States" or "US" to "United States".

3. Fill in missing data: For any missing responses, either fill in the correct data if possible or mark the entry as "N/A."

4. Convert currencies: Convert all salary responses in different currencies (e.g., GBP, CAD) to a single currency (e.g., USD) for consistency and ease of comparison.

5. Remove unnecessary columns: Remove any columns that are not needed for analysis, such as the "If 'Other,' please indicate the currency here:" column.

6. Split combined columns: Split columns that contain more than one variable, such as the "Job title and Responsibility" column, into separate columns for easier analysis.

7. Standardize job titles: Standardize job titles to a more general term for easier categorization and comparison (e.g., change "Change & Internal Communications Manager" to "Communications Manager").

8. Remove special characters and extra spaces: Clean up any special characters or extra spaces in the data.

9. Reorder columns: Reorder columns for easier readability and analysis. For example, group similar columns together (e.g., Age, Work Experience, and Education Level).

10. Validate data: Check for any discrepancies, outliers, or errors in the data and correct them if necessary.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Here's Python code using the pandas library to clean the given data. This assumes the data is in a CSV file named "raw_data.csv". You can adjust the file name and paths as needed.

```python
import pandas as pd

# Read the CSV file
data = pd.read_csv("raw_data.csv")

# Standardize categories
data["Age"] = data["Age"].apply(lambda x: str(x) + " years")
data["Work Experience"] = data["Work Experience"].apply(lambda x: str(x) + " years")

# Correct inconsistencies in text responses
data["Country"] = data["Country"].replace({"USA": "United States", "US": "United States"})
data["Industry"] = data["Industry"].replace("Computing or Tech", "Technology")
data["City"] = data["City"].str.strip()

# Fill in missing data with 'N/A'
data = data.fillna("N/A")

# Convert currencies to USD (Assuming 1 GBP = 1.39 USD and 1 CAD = 0.8 USD)
currency_conversion = {"USD": 1, "GBP": 1.39, "CAD": 0.8}
data["Annual Salary (USD)"] = data["Annual Salary"].astype(float) * data["Currency"].map(currency_conversion)

# Remove unnecessary columns
data = data.drop(columns=["Annual Salary", "Currency", "If “Other,” please indicate the currency here:"])

# Rename and reorder columns for easier readability
data = data.rename(columns={
    "Age": "Age Range",
    "Work Experience": "Work Experience Range",
    "Job title": "Job Title"
})
data = data[[
    "Age Range", "Industry", "Job Title", "Annual Salary (USD)", "Country",
    "City", "Work Experience Range", "Education Level"
]]

# Save cleaned data to a new CSV file
data.to_csv("cleaned_data.csv", index=False)
```

This code will generate a new CSV file named "cleaned_data.csv" with the cleaned data. Note that some data cleaning tasks, such as standardizing job titles, require domain knowledge and may not be easily automated. In those cases, you may need to manually clean the data or use more advanced techniques like natural language processing.
